prompt: Hazard Class 3 covers which materials?

options.text: Flammable liquids
Explosives only (Class 1)
Gases only (Class 2)
Corrosives only (Class 8)

explanation: DOT classes: 1 explosives, 2 gases, 3 flammable liquids, 8 corrosives, etc.

prompt: A placard is primarily used to:

options.text: Replace shipping papers entirely in all cases
Communicate the hazard of materials on a vehicle to emergency responders and the public
Increase fuel economy
Act as a vehicle registration sticker

explanation: Placards provide hazard communication; shipping papers remain required documentation.

prompt: Where must shipping papers for hazardous materials typically be kept while driving?

options.text: Locked in an inaccessible trailer toolbox only
Left at home for safety
Within immediate reach of the driver / readily available (often on the seat or in a holder) when the driver is at the controls
Mailed to the consignee before departure only

explanation: Emergency responders must be able to access shipping papers quickly.

distractorExplanations.b: Papers must travel with the shipment.
distractorExplanations.d: Consignee gets delivery docs; driver needs papers en route.

prompt: The hazardous materials identification number on shipping papers and placards is commonly a:

options.text: Two-letter state code only
Driver license number
Fuel receipt number
Four-digit UN/NA number

explanation: UN/NA numbers identify specific materials for emergency response.

prompt: Division 1.1 explosives are characterized as:

options.text: Mass explosion hazard
No hazard whatsoever
Only combustible solids like Class 4.1 always
Only infectious substances

explanation: 1.1 = mass explosion; other 1.x divisions have different explosive hazards.

prompt: Which placard color is classically associated with flammable liquids (Class 3)?

options.text: White with black stripes only for all classes
Red
Blue only for all flammables
All-green for flammable liquids

explanation: Class 3 flammable placards are red; other classes use different color patterns.

distractorExplanations.a: Stripes appear on certain placards (e.g., dangerous when wet variants), not as the Class 3 standard.
distractorExplanations.c: Blue is associated with dangerous when wet (4.3) among others.

prompt: The Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG) is used to:

options.text: Replace CDL skills testing
Set fuel tax rates
Provide initial isolation/evacuation and fire/spill guidance for hazmat incidents
Calculate payroll

explanation: ERG guides first responders and drivers on immediate protective actions.

prompt: A 'Dangerous' placard may be used when:

options.text: Any single gallon of milk is shipped
Only when carrying Class 7 exclusively always
In place of all shipping papers
Non-bulk packages of two or more hazard categories require placarding and table rules allow the dangerous placard instead of multiple placards

explanation: Dangerous placard rules are specific aggregation/placarding table exceptions—not a universal substitute.

prompt: Hazard Class 8 materials are:

options.text: Corrosives
Explosives
Flammable gases only
Miscellaneous only (Class 9)

explanation: Class 8 = corrosives; Class 9 = miscellaneous hazardous materials.

prompt: On shipping papers, the basic description of a hazardous material generally includes:

options.text: Only the driver’s nickname
Identification number, proper shipping name, hazard class/division, and packing group (when assigned)
Only the truck color
Only the fuel brand

explanation: The basic description sequence is standardized for clarity in emergencies.

prompt: Packing Group I indicates:

options.text: Least danger always
No hazard
Great danger (highest degree of danger among I/II/III when assigned)
Radioactivity only

explanation: PG I = high danger; PG II = medium; PG III = minor danger (when packing groups apply).

prompt: Which material class includes flammable solids?

options.text: Class 3 only
Class 8 only
Class 2.2 only
Class 4 (e.g., 4.1 flammable solids)

explanation: Class 4 covers flammable solids, spontaneously combustible, and dangerous when wet divisions.

prompt: A square-on-point display on a package typically indicates:

options.text: A hazard warning label for the packaged hazmat
That the box is empty of all meaning
That shipping papers are optional
That the product is always food-grade

explanation: Diamond labels communicate package hazards; vehicle placards are larger related marks.

distractorExplanations.d: Hazmat is not defined as food-grade.

prompt: Division 2.3 refers to:

options.text: Flammable liquids
Poison (toxic) gases
Corrosive solids only
ORM-D consumer commodities only historically as 2.3

explanation: 2.1 flammable gas, 2.2 non-flammable gas, 2.3 poison gas.

distractorExplanations.d: ORM-D was a different limited-quantity concept.

prompt: Who is responsible for ensuring hazmat is properly classified and described before offering shipment?

options.text: Only random bystanders
Only fuel station attendants always
The shipper (with carrier responsibilities for acceptance/transport compliance)
Only weather forecasters

explanation: Shippers classify/package/mark/label/describe; carriers verify before transport and comply en route.

prompt: Incompatible hazardous materials must be:

options.text: Segregated according to segregation tables so they do not react dangerously if released
Always mixed to save space
Stacked with food without barriers when leaking
Loaded with no regard to class

explanation: Segregation prevents dangerous interactions (e.g., acids with cyanides).

prompt: When loading packages of hazmat, you should:

options.text: Throw boxes to speed loading
Protect packages from damage, secure them against movement, and follow orientation marks
Ignore 'this side up' arrows
Place heavy items on fragile hazmat without care

explanation: Package integrity and securement prevent leaks and incidents.

prompt: Cargo heaters / burning heaters around certain hazmat are restricted because:

options.text: Heat always makes corrosives safer
Heat removes all placarding duties
Heat/ignition sources can trigger fire or explosion with flammables/explosives
Heat sterilizes Class 7 automatically

explanation: Ignition control is critical near flammables and explosives.

prompt: Bulk packages vs non-bulk packages differ primarily by:

options.text: Color of the truck only
Driver age only
Whether a CDL exists in another state only
Capacity thresholds defined in the HMR (e.g., bulk generally larger capacities)

explanation: HMR define bulk vs non-bulk by volume/weight capacity criteria.

distractorExplanations.b: Age is credentialing, not packaging class.

prompt: Before loading Class 1 explosives, a driver should ensure:

options.text: The vehicle is appropriate, free of ignition hazards, and loaded per quantity/segregation rules
The trailer is full of loose oily rags next to detonators for cushioning
Smoking is done inside the cargo area
Shipping papers are discarded

explanation: Explosives demand strict fire control and compliance.

prompt: Floor of a vehicle used for hazmat should be:

options.text: Covered in spilled product intentionally
Clean, in good condition, and free of nails/screws that could puncture packages
Soft mud only
Littered with broken glass for traction

explanation: Puncture and contamination risks must be controlled before loading.

prompt: Segregation of acids and cyanides is critical because together they can:

options.text: Form harmless table salt only always
Cancel each other’s placards legally
Release toxic hydrogen cyanide gas
Become Class 9 automatically and safe

explanation: Acid + cyanide can liberate HCN—classic incompatible pair.

distractorExplanations.a: Not a neutralization to safety in cargo practice.

prompt: When loading tanks, manhole covers and valves should be:

options.text: Left open for ventilation while driving highway speeds with liquid load always
Removed and stored at home
Loosely chained so they rattle
Closed and secured per procedures; check for leaks

explanation: Containment integrity prevents releases during transit.

prompt: Packages labeled 'Toxic' should be loaded:

options.text: Away from foodstuffs and per segregation/separation rules
On top of employee lunches intentionally
Mixed into the sleeper berth bedding
Opened to 'air out' while driving

explanation: Poison/toxic materials can contaminate food and living spaces.

distractorExplanations.d: Do not open packages in transit.

prompt: A leaking hazmat package discovered during loading should be:

options.text: Loaded quickly to hide the leak
Not loaded; protect people, contain if trained/safe, and notify appropriate parties
Punctured more to equalize pressure
Given to hitchhikers

explanation: Do not accept damaged/leaking packages; follow emergency and carrier procedures.

prompt: For placarded loads, certain railroad crossings require:

options.text: Racing across without looking
Never using brakes near tracks
Stopping (with exceptions) before crossing, listening, and proceeding only when safe—per hazmat/rail rules taught in CDL manuals
Turning off flashers required by law for visibility always as the only step

explanation: Placarded loads have heightened rail-crossing precautions in training materials/regs.

distractorExplanations.d: Observation/stop rules are the key control.

prompt: Doubling up incompatible compressed gas cylinders loosely is unsafe because:

options.text: Gases always cancel hazards when mixed in cargo
Cylinders cannot leak by design ever
Segregation rules never apply to gases
Damaged valves/releases can create fire, asphyxiation, or toxic exposure risks

explanation: Secure and segregate gas cylinders; protect valves.

prompt: Orientation arrows on packages mean:

options.text: Keep package upright as marked unless rules allow otherwise
Turn the package upside down intentionally
Arrows are decorative only
Arrows replace UN numbers

explanation: Liquid closures and vents may depend on upright orientation.

prompt: When carrying hazmat and food products together, you must:

options.text: Always place poisons directly on produce
Comply with contamination prevention and separation rules; some combinations are forbidden
Ignore all food rules if the trailer is refrigerated
Use food as absorbent for spills

explanation: Food contamination prohibitions are strict for poisons and certain hazmats.

distractorExplanations.c: Refrigeration does not waive contamination rules.

prompt: Securement of hazmat cargo is important because movement can:

options.text: Improve classification accuracy
Remove the need for CDL endorsements
Damage packages, open closures, and cause spills or reactions
Automatically upgrade packing groups to safer levels

explanation: Load securement is a core prevention control.

distractorExplanations.d: PG does not change from straps.

prompt: If hazmat is leaking while you are driving and it is safe to do so, you should:

options.text: Park away from people/ignition sources when possible, secure the scene, keep people away, and notify emergency/carrier contacts
Drive faster to the next city to hide it
Ignore it if the smell is strong
Smoke near the leak to test flammability

explanation: Protect life and limit ignition/exposure; use ERG/carrier emergency procedures.

prompt: Tire fires on placarded vehicles are dangerous because:

options.text: Tires cannot burn near hazmat
Heat can involve the cargo and cause a larger fire/explosion/toxic release
Hazmat always extinguishes tire fires
You should always puncture the cargo tank to cool tires

explanation: Stop/address tire issues early; cargo involvement escalates incidents.

prompt: In a hazmat emergency, the shipping papers and ERG help you:

options.text: Calculate employee bonuses
Reset the odometer legally
Identify material hazards and initial protective distances/actions
Cancel environmental laws

explanation: Identification drives correct isolation and notification.

prompt: Parking a vehicle with Division 1.1/1.2 explosives near an open fire is:

options.text: Required for warming the load
Recommended to dry packages
Allowed if you remain in the sleeper
Prohibited / extremely unsafe

explanation: Keep explosives away from fire/ignition sources and follow distance/parking rules.

distractorExplanations.a: Never warm explosives with open flame.
distractorExplanations.c: Presence in sleeper does not make it safe.

prompt: If you break down with hazmat on a highway, you should:

options.text: Warn others, set out emergency triangles as required, and call for assistance—do not leave the scene unprotected
Abandon the vehicle unmarked at night on a curve
Siphon product onto the roadway to lighten the load
Invite the public to sample the cargo

explanation: Traffic control and notification reduce secondary crashes and exposures.

distractorExplanations.c: Releasing product is a spill crime/hazard.

prompt: Smoking around Class 3 flammable liquids is:

options.text: Required before dipping a tank
Forbidden due to ignition risk
Safe if you use matches not lighters
Encouraged to detect leaks by flame

explanation: No smoking/open flame near flammables.

distractorExplanations.a: Never introduce ignition to check tanks casually.
distractorExplanations.c: Any flame source is a risk.

prompt: When contacting emergency responders about a spill, prioritize giving:

options.text: Your favorite sports team
Only the trailer paint code
Location, material identity/UN number if known, quantity/conditions, and injuries/hazards observed
A guess without checking papers when papers are available

explanation: Accurate identity and location enable correct response.

prompt: A white 'toxic inhalation hazard' concern means responders should:

options.text: Ignore wind direction
Assume the material is always harmless air
Use water streams randomly without guidance always
Emphasize respiratory protection and isolation downwind per ERG guidance

explanation: TIH materials demand atmospheric precautions; follow ERG/specialist advice.

prompt: Fires involving water-reactive materials (4.3) may worsen if you:

options.text: Apply water inappropriately, generating flammable/toxic gases
Only use the correct extinguishing method from guidance
Evacuate as directed
Shut off ignition sources when safe

explanation: Water can react violently with some materials—use ERG/specialist methods.

prompt: After a minor non-threatening fueling splash of diesel on skin, general first action is:

options.text: Inject the diesel
Remove contaminated clothing as needed and wash skin with soap/water; seek care if irritation persists
Ignite the clothing to burn it off
Drink solvent to 'neutralize'

explanation: Decontaminate skin; avoid absurd harmful 'remedies'.

prompt: Route restrictions for certain hazmat exist to:

options.text: Shorten all deliveries regardless of law
Avoid weigh stations only for fun
Keep high-risk loads away from tunnels, bridges, or densely populated preferred routes as required
Eliminate the need for placards

explanation: Follow designated/preferred routes and prohibitions for the material and jurisdiction.

prompt: If shipping papers and placards disagree, you should:

options.text: Always trust the prettier placard and leave
Invent a new UN number
Cover placards with duct tape and go
Not move the shipment until discrepancies are resolved with shipper/carrier procedures

explanation: Identification must be accurate before transport continues.

prompt: Emergency response information on/with shipping papers must be:

options.text: Available for the materials transported (e.g., ERG pages or equivalent emergency info)
Optional poetry
Only a blank page
Stored exclusively overseas

explanation: Emergency response information is required to accompany hazmat shipments.

distractorExplanations.d: Must be available with the shipment.

prompt: When evacuating an area for a hazmat release, a key principle is:

options.text: Gather crowds at the leak for photos
Move people upwind / to safe distances per guidance and keep them away from vapors
Shelter inside the vapor cloud
Drive through the spill to 'disperse' it

explanation: Protect by distance, wind direction, and denying entry.

prompt: A driver who discovers a suspected radioactive package with damaged integrity should:

options.text: Open it to inspect the contents closely for a long time
Carry fragments in a pocket
Minimize time near the package, maximize distance/shielding, isolate the area, and notify authorities/carrier
Ignore radiation symbols

explanation: Time-distance-shielding and expert response apply to radiological concerns.

prompt: Hazmat security awareness training is required so employees can:

options.text: Recognize and reduce security risks such as theft, tampering, or terrorism targeting hazmat
Ignore suspicious activity
Disable locks for convenience
Post tank access codes publicly

explanation: Security plans/awareness reduce intentional misuse of hazardous materials.

prompt: A suspicious person photographing a chlorine tank terminal for no legitimate reason should prompt:

options.text: Giving them valve keys
Reporting to security/law enforcement per site procedures
Inviting them into restricted areas
Helping them climb the tank

explanation: Unusual surveillance of hazmat sites is a reportable security concern.

prompt: Selective transportation security measures for certain high-risk hazmat include:

options.text: Leaving loaded explosives trucks unlocked downtown overnight intentionally
Removing all seals from high-risk loads for convenience
Enhanced security plans, attendance/securement, and route/communication practices as required
Posting shipment schedules of high-risk loads on public social media

explanation: Higher-risk materials trigger stronger security plan requirements.

distractorExplanations.a: Unsecured high-risk loads are forbidden practice.

prompt: Enroute security includes:

options.text: Picking up unknown hitchhikers into the cargo area
Leaving keys in the ignition at every truck stop
Discussing load contents loudly with strangers
Minimizing stops, locking vehicles, and being aware of tailing or tampering

explanation: Reduce opportunities for theft/hijack of hazmat.

prompt: A broken cargo seal that you did not expect may indicate:

options.text: Possible tampering—follow company procedures, document, and report before proceeding blindly
Automatic proof the load is safer
A reason to ignore inventory checks
Permission to remove remaining seals

explanation: Seal integrity is a security control; anomalies need response.

prompt: CDL hazmat endorsement generally requires:

options.text: Only a verbal promise with no vetting
TSA security threat assessment / fingerprint-based background check process (as applicable) plus knowledge testing
No identification documents ever
Only a forklift wallet card

explanation: Federal security threat assessment is part of hazmat endorsement issuance.

prompt: Employees should report threats or suspicious purchases of precursors when:

options.text: Never, because reporting is discouraged
Only after an explosion has occurred
Behavior suggests planning to misuse hazardous materials
Only if the person is a friend

explanation: Early reporting can prevent incidents; follow company/law enforcement channels.

distractorExplanations.d: Relationship does not bar reporting crimes.

prompt: Access to hazmat storage areas should be:

options.text: Open to the public 24/7
Shared on a flyer with gate codes
Available to anyone who asks politely once
Limited to authorized personnel with controls (locks, badges, escorts as required)

explanation: Physical access control is a core security measure.

prompt: Which communication practice is more secure for high-risk load details?

options.text: Need-to-know sharing over secure company channels
Broadcasting UN numbers and routes on public CB for entertainment
Posting manifests on public forums
Texting strangers the gate code

explanation: Limit sensitive logistics details to authorized parties.

prompt: Vehicle attendance requirements for certain explosives/hazmat mean:

options.text: The vehicle can always be abandoned for days with keys in it
Someone qualified may need to attend the vehicle / keep it within view per the specific rule set
Attendance is never required for any Class 1
Attendance means watching TV at home while the truck is downtown

explanation: Attendance/surveillance rules reduce theft and unauthorized access—know the material-specific requirements.

distractorExplanations.c: Some Class 1 loads have strict attendance rules.
distractorExplanations.d: Attendance means with the vehicle as defined.

prompt: If you suspect your placarded load is being followed by an unknown vehicle engaging in hostile surveillance, you should:

options.text: Ram the vehicle immediately as first step always
Stop in a deserted area to confront them alone
Avoid confrontation, note details, and contact law enforcement/carrier security per procedure
Hand over the shipping papers to the unknown driver

explanation: Personal safety and reporting beat vigilantism.

distractorExplanations.d: Do not surrender docs to unknowns.

prompt: Tamper-evident devices help by:

options.text: Increasing payload weight illegally
Replacing driver training
Eliminating the need for locks entirely
Showing whether unauthorized access to cargo may have occurred

explanation: Seals complement locks; they detect intrusion attempts.

prompt: A security plan for hazmat employers typically addresses:

options.text: Personnel security, unauthorized access, and en-route security measures
Only cafeteria menus
Only paint colors of offices
Only vacation photos

explanation: Plans systematically reduce risks of theft/misuse.

prompt: Carrying unauthorized passengers or allowing unknown persons into the cargo area of a hazmat load is:

options.text: Recommended for hospitality
A serious security and safety violation
Required by placarding rules
A substitute for shipping papers

explanation: Control of the vehicle and cargo is fundamental security.

distractorExplanations.c: Placards do not require strangers aboard.

prompt: Which action best supports both safety and security at delivery?

options.text: Leave the trailer unlocked on a street corner for 'whoever claims it'
Accept a verbal 'I'm from the company' with no verification for high-risk loads
Verify receiver authorization, check seals/IDs per procedure, and document exceptions
Remove placards before arrival to avoid attention while still loaded

explanation: Identity verification and documentation prevent diversion.

distractorExplanations.d: Placards stay until unload rules allow removal.
